explanations.13: The 'declare -a' command creates an array variable named pids; the '-a' flag tells bash this will hold multiple values instead of just one.
explanations.15: This resets pids to an empty array, which might seem redundant after line 13 but ensures it's definitely empty in case the function runs multiple times.
explanations.22: The 'nproc' command returns how many CPU cores your machine has; this line multiplies that by 2 and stores the result in NUMBER_OF_THREADS.
explanations.33: The '&' at the end runs the demo function in the background, meaning the script continues immediately without waiting for demo to finish.
explanations.38: The '$!' variable holds the process ID (a unique number) of the last background job we started; '+=' adds it to the pids array.
explanations.43: The 'jobs' command lists all running background processes; this line checks if we have as many or more running jobs than our thread limit.
explanations.47: The 'wait' command pauses the script until the background process with that specific PID finishes.
explanations.70: The '$(date +%s)' command gets the current time in seconds since 1970; this is used to measure how long the script takes to run.
explanations.73: This subtracts the start time from the end time to calculate total elapsed seconds.
